summary: Patrick reports major progress on Bingo Card Creator 3.0's web application, specifically the PDF generation for printing bingo cards. After years of struggling with Java's primitive printing facilities, he found the Ruby Prawn library produced better results in 8 hours than he achieved in 2 years with Java. He outlines remaining work including user management, copy rewriting, and integration challenges, while noting his biggest concern is whether non-technical users will pay for a web application.
key_points: Switching from Java Swing canvas printing to PDF generation via Ruby's Prawn library dramatically improved output quality and reduced code complexity to a quarter of the original size
The web version solves long-requested features (titles, watermarks) that were too difficult to implement in the Java codebase due to accumulated technical debt
The biggest remaining risk isn't technical but commercial: convincing non-technical users that a web application is worth paying for
He plans to keep the desktop version alongside the web app rather than deprecating it, hedging against user resistance to paying for web software
money_quotes: I've got better behavior out of it in 8 hours of playing around then I've been able to coax out of BCC in 2 years.
There is still some ugly spaghetti code hiding in the bowels of it, but its literally a quarter of the size of the stuff in BCC itself, and much, much more comprehensible.
My biggest worry is the perennial one for web applications: can I convince non-technical users that "web sites" are worth paying money for?
This is one of the main reasons I'm not planning on deprecating the downloadable version anytime soon.
I don't want someone printing 800 cards for a Fortune 500 retreat to lock up the server accidentally, but I do want to support that use case.
key_insight: Choosing the right technology stack can compress years of painful workarounds into hours of productive development, but the hardest problems in software products are often commercial rather than technical.
